nodes.title: Cli-Fi and Enviro Humanities
nodes.description: Advanced textual analyses that broaden the concept of text to include music, film and television, the visual arts, popular culture and everyday meanings and practices. May be repeated for credit with different content. Topics vary by offering; periodic offering at intervals that may exceed four years. Prerequisite: Graduate student.
nodes.other_requirements: Graduate student standing only. Students must attend class, keep up with the readings, and participate in seminar discussions.

nodes.title: Charles Dickens
nodes.description: In-depth study of the works, critical reception, and context of an author writing in English. Representative topics: Chaucer; Shakespeare; Milton; Austen; Dickinson; Morrison. May be repeated for credit with different content. Topics vary by offering; periodic offering at intervals that may exceed four years. Prerequisite: Graduate student.

nodes.title: Literary Journalism
nodes.description: Intermediate-level creative writing course. Representative topics: Literary Journalism, Travel Writing; Art of Adaptation. May repeat for credit with different content. Topics vary by offering; periodic offering at intervals may exceed four years. Prerequisites: ENGL 1027, ENGL 1700, ENGL 1702, ENGL 1705, ENGL 1730, or ENGL 1740; minimum Sophomore standing.
nodes.other_requirements: Minimum Sophomore standing; PACE students with permission and override

nodes.title: Gender Sexuality&Environ Lit
nodes.description: Intensive instruction and practice in writing, reading, research, and revision through the exploration of a theme related to the instructor's expertise. Topics vary by offering; periodic offering at intervals that may exceed four years.
nodes.other_requirements: Sophomores, juniors and seniors only. PACE students by permission and override.

nodes.title: Bible, Bibles, and Biblical
nodes.description: An introduction to the historical study of religion and its relation to written texts through an examination of the creation of biblical and related writings of ancient Israel, Egypt, Assyria, Babylon, and the early Christian movement. Representative topics: What is the Bible; Literary and Historical Perspectives on the Bible. May repeat for credit with different content. Topics vary by offering; periodic offering at intervals that may exceed four years. Cross-listed with: REL 1250.

nodes.title: Milton
nodes.description: Milton's major works in various intellectual, historical, and aesthetic contexts, with special attention to Paradise Lost. Prerequisites: Three hours in English numbered 1010 to 1990; minimum Sophomore standing.
nodes.other_requirements: Minimum Sophomore standing. PACE students by permission and override.

nodes.title: IS:20th C Women's Lit
nodes.description: A course which is tailored to fit the interests of a specific student, which occurs outside the traditional classroom/laboratory setting under the supervision of a faculty member, for which credit is awarded. Offered at department discretion.
nodes.other_requirements: Instructor permission required

nodes.title: Playwriting
nodes.description: Studies models of dramatic structure and contemporary concepts of writing for the stage. Students will apply these principles to the creation of original works. May be repeated once for credit. Prerequisites: ENGL 1027, ENGL 1730, or THE 1500; minimum Sophomore standing. Cross-listed with: THE 2600.
nodes.other_requirements: minimum Sophomore standing. PACE students by permission and override

nodes.title: Writing Place in CNF
nodes.description: Representative topics include Forms of Journalism and Writing for the Web. May be repeated for credit with different content. Topics vary by offering; periodic offering at intervals that may exceed four years.

nodes.title: British Literature I
nodes.description: Selected texts from the beginnings to the late 18th century. Explores periodization, genre, key terms and concepts through close reading and critical analysis. Fulfills major requirements; open to non-majors.

nodes.title: Lit & Society in Africa
nodes.description: Examines trends in contemporary African literature and relationship to other traditions. Representative topics: African Drama; African Fiction; African Poetry. May repeat for credit with different content. Topics vary by offering; periodic offering at intervals that may exceed four years. Prerequisites: Three hours in English numbered 1010 to 1990; minimum Sophomore standing.
nodes.other_requirements: Minimum Sophomore standing; PACE students by permission and override

nodes.title: Master's Thesis Research
nodes.description: Research leading toward completion of the Master's Thesis.
nodes.other_requirements: Graduate standing only; Instructor permission

nodes.title: Real., Regional., Naturalism
nodes.description: Advanced study in 18th- and 19th-century literature. Representative topics: Romantic Poetry and Poetics; Mary Shelley and Her Circle; The Gothic. May be repeated for credit with different content. Topics vary by offering; periodic offering at intervals that may exceed four years. Credit not awarded for both ENGL 3350 and ENGL 5300. Prerequisites: ENGL 1500, ENGL 2000; English major, Secondary Education major with a concentration in English, or English minor; minimum Junior standing.
nodes.other_requirements: English major, Secondary Education major with a concentration in English, or English minor; minimum Junior standing.

nodes.title: Classic Monsters
nodes.description: Exploration of topics in theme and genre. May be repeated for credit with different content. Topics vary by offering; periodic offering at intervals that may exceed four years. Credit not awarded for both ENGL 5100 and ENGL 3100.

nodes.title: The Films of Stephen King
nodes.description: See Schedule of Courses for specific titles. May be repeated for credit with different content.

nodes.title: The Art of the Essay
nodes.description: In this intermediate writing course, students explore and practice variations in the genre known as the nonfiction essay, attending to audience, purpose, context, style, and medium.

nodes.title: American Literature I
nodes.description: Selected texts from the beginnings to the Civil War. Explores periodization, genre, key terms and concepts through close reading and critical analysis. Fulfills major requirements; open to non-majors.

nodes.title: Modern Drama
nodes.description: Twentieth-century drama by writers such as Ibsen, Shaw, Beckett, Brecht, Miller, Pinter, and Churchill. Prerequisites: Three hours in English numbered 1010 to 1990; minimum Sophomore standing.
nodes.other_requirements: Minimum Sophomore standing. PACE students with permission and override

nodes.title: FYS:Gender, Sexu. & Enviro Lit
nodes.description: Intensive first-year seminar focused on specific themes and/or disciplinary perspectives. Emphasis on developing critical reading and writing skills, substantive revision, information literacy, and analytical thinking. First-year seminars are frequently organized to meet one of the disciplinary Catamount Core requirements. Topics vary by offering; periodic offering at intervals that may exceed four years.

nodes.title: Honors
nodes.description: College honors thesis or other department/program honors, under the supervision of a faculty member. Offered at department discretion.

nodes.title: Af Am Sci Fi/Fantasy
nodes.description: Representative topic: African-American Women Writers in the Twentieth- and Twenty-first Centuries. May be repeated for credit with different content. Topics vary by offering; periodic offering at intervals that may exceed four years. Cross-listed with: CRES 1825.

nodes.title: British Literature II
nodes.description: Selected texts from the late 18th century to the present. Explores periodization, genre, key terms and concepts through close reading and critical analysis. Fulfills major requirements; open to non-majors.

nodes.title: Writing Autofiction
nodes.description: Advanced study in writing practice, craft, and theory. Representative topics: Innovations in Life Writing; Protest and Persuasion; Stories of the Body. May be repeated for credit with different content. Topics vary by offering; periodic offering at intervals that may exceed four years. Prerequisites: ENGL 2702, ENGL 2740, ENGL 2750, ENGL 2760, or ENGL 2770; English major, Secondary Education major with a concentration in English, English minor, or Writing minor; minimum Junior standing.
nodes.other_requirements: English major, Secondary Education major with a concentration in English, English minor, or Writing minor; minimum Junior standing. ENGL majors, minors & SEE majors with concentration in ENGL only minimum Junior standing. Students must be advanced and have written some fiction before. They should be familiar with various genres and traditions of fiction and literary history.

nodes.title: LASP: Writing The Manuscript
nodes.description: Intensive course in a broad disciplinary area (humanities, social sciences, arts, or natural sciences). Part of an integrated first-year experience in which students take 2-4 classes exploring aesthetic, humanistic, social, linguistic, environmental, or scientific issues. May repeat for credit with different content. Topics vary by offering; periodic offering at intervals that may exceed four years. Co-requisite: Enrollment in the appropriate Liberal Arts  Scholars Program.
nodes.other_requirements: Co-requisite: Enrollment in the appropriate Liberal Arts Scholars Program. LASP Arts Scholars only; Instructor permission required

nodes.title: US Latino Writers
nodes.description: Study of texts written by Latinos since the 1960s. Topics: construction of ethnic identities, representation of race/gender relations; writers and their communities. Prerequisite: Three hours in English numbered 1010 to 1990; minimum Sophomore standing. Cross-listed with: CRES 2827.
nodes.other_requirements: Minimum sophomore standing. PACE students with permission and override

nodes.title: Intro to Literary Studies
nodes.description: Introduction to the critical work of close reading across literary genres, understanding of key terms and concepts, and writing in the discipline. Required for English majors and minors. Topics vary by section.
nodes.other_requirements: Junior and senior ENGL, SEE majors and ENGL minors only until Wednesday, April 8th; then ENGL SEE majors and ENGL minors only during the first week registration. Open to Degree and PACE students. Full class participation (attendance AND involvement in class discussions and in-class writing activities) is important and expected.

nodes.title: Woolf, Faulkner, & Morrison
nodes.description: Representative topics: Joyce. May repeat for credit with different content. Topics vary by offering; periodic offering at intervals that may exceed four years. Prerequisites: Three hours in English numbered 1010 to 1990; minimum Sophomore standing.
nodes.other_requirements: minimum Sophomore standing. PACE students by permission and override

nodes.title: Gender Sexuality& Environment
nodes.description: Courses examine the representation and construction of gender and sexuality in literature. Representative topics: Gender, Sexuality and Identity in American Poetry. May be repeated for credit with different content. Topics vary by offering; periodic offering at intervals that may exceed four years. Cross-listed with: GSWS 1620.

nodes.title: Women's Literature
nodes.description: Survey of women's literary tradition in English. Focuses on the ways women have written, read, written about, and been represented in nineteenth- and twentieth-century literature. Cross-listed with: GSWS 1630.

nodes.title: Writing Poetry
nodes.description: This upper-level course for poets of proven ability employs a seminar/workshop format, with most classroom time devoted to manuscript discussion. May be repeated once for credit. Prerequisites: ENGL 1027, ENGL 1700, ENGL 1702, ENGL 1705, ENGL 1730, or ENGL 1740; minimum Sophomore standing.
nodes.other_requirements: minimum Sophomore standing; PACE students by permission and override

nodes.title: Intro to Creative Writing
nodes.description: Introductory course on techniques of writing poetry, short prose fiction, and creative nonfiction. Classes organized around discussion of student work; weekly writing assignments.

nodes.title: Great American Race Novel
nodes.description: Topics address the concept of race and/or the contributions of ethnically diverse writers to American culture. Focus and readings vary. May repeat for credit with different content. Topics vary by offering; periodic offering at intervals that may exceed four years. Prerequisites: Three hours in English numbered 1010 to 1990; minimum Sophomore standing. Cross-listed with: CRES 2650.
nodes.other_requirements: minimum Sophomore standing

nodes.title: CL:Exploring Writing Centers
nodes.description: A continuation of ENGL 2790, this course explores theoretical frameworks for writing centers and how they can shape ways tutors respond to writers. Prerequisite: ENGL 2790; Instructor permission.
nodes.other_requirements: Instructor permission. Students must have been selected as tutors for the Undergraduate Writing Center.

nodes.title: Internship
nodes.description: On-site supervised work experience combined with a structured academic learning plan directed by faculty member or faculty-staff team with a faculty member as instructor of record, for which academic credit is awarded. Departmental permission required. Offered at department discretion.
nodes.other_requirements: Departmental permission required. Offered at department discretion. Instructor permission required.

nodes.title: Tolkien:Gender/Race, the Other
nodes.description: Introductory courses addressing the representation and construction of race in literature and/or the contributions of ethnically diverse writers to the American culture. May repeat for credit with different content. Topics vary by offering; periodic offering at intervals that may exceed four years. Cross-listed with: CRES 1821.

nodes.title: American Post-Modernism
nodes.description: Interdisciplinary topics examining literature and cultures of the Post-Modern condition. Representative topics include: Magical Realism, Realism, and Hyper-realism. May repeat for credit with different content. Topics vary by offering; periodic offering at intervals that may exceed four years. Prerequisites: Three hours in English numbered 1010 to 1990; minimum Sophomore standing.
nodes.other_requirements: Minimum Sophomore standing. PACE students with permission and override

nodes.title: Piers Plowman
nodes.description: Topics examining Medieval literature in various intellectual, historical, and aesthetic contexts. Representative topics: Medieval Drama; Daughters of Mary/Daughters of Eve. May repeat for credit with different content. Topics vary by offering; periodic offering at intervals that may exceed four years. Prerequisites: Three hours in English numbered 1010 to 1990; minimum Sophomore standing.
nodes.other_requirements: minimum Sophomore standing

nodes.title: Surv of Lit Theory & Criticism
nodes.description: Topics in Theory treats intensively a focused area of theoretical inquiry. May be repeated with different content. Topics vary by offering; periodic offering at intervals that may exceed four years. Prerequisite: Graduate student.
nodes.other_requirements: Graduate student. Graduate students only

nodes.title: Modern Poetry
nodes.description: Study of poetry as a genre. Specific sections may focus on a particular theme, author, or time period. May repeat for credit with different content. Topics vary by offering; periodic offering at intervals that may exceed four years. Prerequisites: Three hours in English numbered 1010 to 1990; minimum Sophomore standing.
nodes.other_requirements: Minimum Sophomore standing. PACE students by permission and override

nodes.title: Invention of American Nature
nodes.description: Interdisciplinary topics examining issues in nineteenth-century American culture. Representative topics include: Dissent in America, American Literary Cultures. May repeat for credit with different content. Topics vary by offering; periodic offering at intervals that may exceed four years. Prerequisites: Three hours in English numbered 1010 to 1990; minimum Sophomore standing.
nodes.other_requirements: Minimum Sophomore standing. PACE students by permission and override

nodes.title: Performance Beyond The Human
nodes.description: Advanced study in literary genres, forms, and themes. Representative topics: Noir in Fiction and Film; Great American Race Novel; Post-Apocalyptic Fiction. May be repeated for credit with different content. Topics vary by offering; periodic offering at intervals that may exceed four years. Credit not awarded for both ENGL 3100 and ENGL 5100. Prerequisites: ENGL 1500, ENGL 2000; English major, Secondary Education with a concentration in English, or English minor; minimum Junior standing.
nodes.other_requirements: English major, Secondary Education with a concentration in English, or English minor; minimum Junior standing.

nodes.title: Jazz& The Cultural Imagination
nodes.description: Interdisciplinary topics in African American literature and culture. Representative topics include: The Harlem Renaissance and Negritude; Publishing Blackness. May repeat for credit with different content. Topics vary by offering; periodic offering at intervals that may exceed four years. Prerequisite: Three hours in English numbered 1010 to 1990; minimum Sophomore standing. Cross-listed with: CRES 2670.
nodes.other_requirements: minimum Sophomore standing; PACE students with permission and override

nodes.title: Writing Fiction
nodes.description: This upper-level course for fiction writers of proven ability employs a seminar/workshop format, with most classroom time devoted to manuscript discussion. May be repeated once for credit. Prerequisites: ENGL 1027, ENGL 1700, ENGL 1702, ENGL 1705, ENGL 1730, or ENGL 1740; minimum Sophomore standing.
nodes.other_requirements: Minimum Sophomore standing. PACE students by permission and override

nodes.title: Introduction to Drama
nodes.description: Study of the play as a work of literature and as a dramatic experience. Continental, British, and American drama from all ages.

nodes.title: Literary Theory
nodes.description: Survey of literary and cultural theory introducing a variety of major approaches to the interpretation of literature. Required for all English majors and minors. Pre/Co-requisite: ENGL 1500.
nodes.other_requirements: Junior and senior ENGL, SEE majors and ENGL minors only until Wednesday, April 8th; then ENGL SEE majors and ENGL minors only during the first week registration

nodes.title: Haunted World, Shirley Jackson
nodes.description: Subjects vary by semester. Representative topic: Reading the American Wilderness. May be repeated for credit with different content. Topics vary by offering; periodic offering at intervals that may exceed four years.

nodes.title: Stories of the Body
nodes.description: Topics vary by semester and professor. Representative topics: Writing Literary Criticism; Reading and Writing Autobiography; Literary Journalism. May repeat for credit with different content. Topics vary by offering; periodic offering at intervals that may exceed four years. Prerequisites: ENGL 1027, ENGL 1700, ENGL 1702, ENGL 1705, ENGL 1730, or ENGL 1740; minimum Sophomore standing.
nodes.other_requirements: minimum Sophomore standing. PACE students by permission and override

nodes.title: Dramatic Analysis
nodes.description: Examination of structural characteristics of the basic forms and styles of drama and the manner in which they affect theatrical representation.
nodes.other_requirements: Open to degree and PACE students.

nodes.title: Queer and Trans Theory
nodes.description: Advanced study in literary and cultural theory. Representative topics: Feminist Memory; Re-disciplining the History of Literature and the Literature of History. May be repeated for credit with different content. Topics vary by offering; periodic offering at intervals that may exceed four years. Credit not awarded for both ENGL 3010 and ENGL 5010. Prerequisites: ENGL 1500, ENGL 2000; English major, Secondary Education with a concentration in English, or English minor; minimum Junior standing.
nodes.other_requirements: English major, Secondary Education with a concentration in English, or English minor; minimum Junior standing. PACE students with permission and override

nodes.title: Internship
nodes.description: On-site supervised work experience combined with a structured academic learning plan directed by a faculty member or a faculty-staff team in which a faculty member is the instructor of record, for which academic credit is awarded. Offered at department discretion.
nodes.other_requirements: Instructor permission required

nodes.title: Evolution of Modern Fantasy
nodes.description: Topics in Science Fiction and Fantasy Literature. Subjects vary by semester. Representative topics: Tolkien's Middle Earth; The Hobbit; Survey of Science Fiction and Fantasy. May be repeated for credit with different content. Topics vary by offering; periodic offering at intervals that may exceed four years.

nodes.title: Written Expression
nodes.description: A foundational composition course featuring a sequence of writing, reading, and information literacy assignments. Students learn to write and revise for different rhetorical situations while increasing their mastery of academic conventions. Some sections designed for specific student audiences.

nodes.title: CL:Tutoring Writing
nodes.description: Students who will be tutoring at the Writing Center explore ways of responding to writers one-on-one. Prerequisites: Minimum Sophomore standing, Instructor permission.
nodes.other_requirements: Minimum Sophomore standing, Instructor permission.

nodes.title: American Literature II
nodes.description: Selected texts from end of Civil War to the present. Explores periodization, genre, key terms and concepts through close reading and critical analysis. Fulfills major requirements; open to non-majors.
